critic_raw:
The Memory Module's core claim is that it reduces CNN representation capacity specifically for anomalous inputs, but this mechanism conflates two distinct phenomena: the memory bottleneck forcing reconstruction through prototypical patterns (the intended mechanism) and ordinary encoder-decoder reconstruction error that would occur regardless of the memory module's presence. Without an ablation comparing the full model against a plain autoencoder with matched capacity, the separateness and compactness losses cannot be credited for any performance improvement — the capacity reduction from the bottleneck alone might account for the gains.

The hybrid Abnormality Score combining PSNR and L2 loss has a fundamental double-counting problem. PSNR is a monotonic function of MSE (equivalently L2), so combining them is not complementary fusion of independent signals — it is a nonlinear re-weighting of the same underlying error signal. The weighting between these two components is not principled from the anomaly detection objective, yet the choice directly controls threshold sensitivity. If the authors tuned this weighting on the test set (even implicitly by selecting the combination that best separated normal from anomalous), AUC estimates will be inflated.

Test-Time Updation of memory items introduces a direct train-test boundary violation. If memory items are updated using inference-time observations before the anomaly score is computed, the model is conditioning its anomaly detector on the very distribution it is evaluating. For a video sequence that starts normal and transitions to anomalous, the update strategy would adaptively normalize toward the early-sequence statistics, which could either help or hurt depending on update timing — but the paper's framing treats this as unambiguously beneficial. The evaluation protocol must specify whether anomaly scores are computed before or after memory updates per frame, and whether the updating is online (sequential) or batch. These choices change the task from detection to post-hoc classification.

The proxy tasks (reconstruction vs. future-frame prediction) conflate different assumptions about what constitutes a 'normal' pattern. Reconstruction proxy assumes stationarity of normal patterns; prediction proxy assumes temporal regularity. The paper combines them without establishing that the learned memory items are aligned across both objectives. If memory items encode good reconstructors but poor predictors, the hybrid score will produce contradictory anomaly signals that the scalar combination cannot resolve.
all_issues_raised: No ablation separates the contribution of the Memory Module from a plain capacity-matched autoencoder — the separateness/compactness losses and the memory bottleneck cannot be individually credited for AUC improvements.

The hybrid PSNR+L2 Abnormality Score double-counts reconstruction error because PSNR is a monotonic transform of MSE; the weighting between them is not principled and if tuned against the test distribution it inflates AUC estimates.

Test-Time Updation violates the train-test boundary: updating memory items from inference-time frames before computing anomaly scores conflates model adaptation with evaluation, and the protocol for when scores are computed relative to updates is not specified.

Combining reconstruction and prediction proxy tasks in the same memory module conflates temporal stationarity with temporal regularity as the definition of 'normality', producing potentially contradictory anomaly signals that a scalar combination cannot resolve.
all_issues_adjudicated: No ablation separates the contribution of the Memory Module from a plain capacity-matched autoencoder — the separateness/compactness losses and the memory bottleneck cannot be individually credited for AUC improvements.

The hybrid PSNR+L2 Abnormality Score double-counts reconstruction error because PSNR is a monotonic transform of MSE; the weighting between them is not principled and if tuned against the test distribution it inflates AUC estimates.

Test-Time Updation violates the train-test boundary: updating memory items from inference-time frames before computing anomaly scores conflates model adaptation with evaluation, and the protocol for when scores are computed relative to updates is not specified.

Combining reconstruction and prediction proxy tasks in the same memory module conflates temporal stationarity with temporal regularity as the definition of 'normality', producing potentially contradictory anomaly signals that a scalar combination cannot resolve.
